corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: In this section, we propose an optimized training approach to dense passage retrieval for opendomain QA, namely RocketQA.
We first introduce the background of the dual-encoder architecture, and then describe the three novel training strategies in RocketQA.
We first introduce the background of the dual-encoder architecture, and then describe the three novel training strategies in RocketQA.
We develop our passage retriever based on the typical dual-encoder architecture, as illustrated in Figure .
Next, we propose three improved training strategies to address the three challenges.
As illustrated at the top of Figure 2, we present an example for in-batch negatives when training on A GPUs in a data parallel way.
To further optimize the training with more negatives, we propose to use cross-batch negatives when training on multiple GPUs, as illustrated at the bottom of Figure .
Because the crossencoder architecture is more powerful for capturing semantic similarity via deep interaction and shows much better performance than the dual-encoder ar- 1.
Hence, we first train a cross-encoder (following the architecture shown in Figure ).
Following previous work, we use MRR and Recall at top k ranks to evaluate the performance of passage retrieval, and exact match (EM) to measure the performance of answer extraction.
Following previous work, we use MRR and Recall at top k ranks to evaluate the performance of passage retrieval, and exact match (EM) to measure the performance of answer extraction.
MRR The Reciprocal Rank (RR) calculates the reciprocal of the rank at which the first relevant passage was retrieved.
Recall at top k ranks The top-k recall of a retriever is defined as the proportion of questions to which the top k retrieved passages contain answers.
We manually evaluated the selected data, and the accuracy was higher than 90%.
We also show the performance of endto-end QA based on our retriever on NQ dataset.
Lastly, we present the whole training procedure of RocketQA.
When averaged across questions, it is called Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR).
Typically, the dual-encoder architecture is adopted to learn dense representations of questions and passages for semantic matching.

However, it is difficult to effectively train a dual-encoder due to the challenges including the discrepancy between training and inference, the existence of unlabeled positives and limited training data.
To address these challenges, we propose an optimized training approach, called RocketQA, to improving dense passage retrieval.
The experiment results show that RocketQA significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art models on both MS-MARCO and Natural Questions.
The experiment results show that RocketQA significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art models on both MS-MARCO and Natural Questions.
As the recall component, the first-stage retriever significantly affects the final QA performance.

To deal with the issue of term mismatch, the dual-encoder architecture (as shown in Figure ) has been widely explored to learn dense representations of questions and passages in an end-to-end manner, which provides better representations for semantic matching.
However, it is still difficult to effectively train a dual-encoder for dense passage retrieval due to the following three major challenges.
